report_name: TeamPCP's Supply Chain Attack Cascade Hits LiteLLM, Stealing AI Credentials

threat_actors.name: TeamPCP
threat_actors.description: A sophisticated threat actor group that executed a multi-stage supply chain attack, compromising the release infrastructure of open-source tools like Trivy, Checkmarx KICS, and LiteLLM to steal credentials from CI/CD pipelines and AI gateway users.
threat_actors.goals: To steal sensitive credentials, including SSH keys, cloud provider tokens, and AI API keys, by compromising widely-used open-source developer tools and their dependencies.

attack_patterns.name: Valid Accounts
attack_patterns.description: TeamPCP initiated the attack by using credentials from a previous, insufficiently remediated security incident to gain access to Trivy's release infrastructure.

attack_patterns.name: Compromise Software Dependencies and Development Tools
attack_patterns.description: The attackers gained control over the GitHub Actions repositories for Trivy and Checkmarx KICS, manipulating version tags to inject malicious code into the CI/CD pipelines of downstream projects.

attack_patterns.name: Compromise Software Supply Chain
attack_patterns.description: TeamPCP published trojanized versions (1.82.7, 1.82.8) of the 'litellm' package to the Python Package Index (PyPI) to distribute their credential-stealing malware.

attack_patterns.name: Command and Scripting Interpreter: Unix Shell
attack_patterns.description: Malicious shell scripts were executed within the CI/CD build environments of projects using the compromised GitHub Actions.

attack_patterns.name: Command and Scripting Interpreter: Python
attack_patterns.description: The malicious payload within the compromised LiteLLM packages was written in Python and embedded in the 'proxy_server.py' file.

attack_patterns.name: Unsecured Credentials
attack_patterns.description: The malware injected into CI/CD pipelines was designed to steal secrets from the build environment, including SSH keys and cloud provider tokens.

attack_patterns.name: Exfiltration Over C2 Channel
attack_patterns.description: Stolen AI API keys and other secrets were exfiltrated from compromised LiteLLM instances to the attacker-controlled C2 server at models.litellm.cloud.

malware.name: TeamPCP Credential Stealer
malware.description: A credential-stealing malware payload injected into CI/CD pipelines via compromised GitHub Actions for Trivy and Checkmarx KICS, and later embedded into trojanized versions of the LiteLLM PyPI package. It was designed to steal SSH keys, cloud tokens, and AI API keys.

vulnerabilities.name: CVE-2026-33634
vulnerabilities.description: A critical vulnerability presumably exploited by TeamPCP to gain initial access to the Trivy release infrastructure, as part of a previously disclosed but insufficiently remediated security incident.
vulnerabilities.kev_required_action: Apply mitigations per vendor instructions, follow applicable BOD 22-01 guidance for cloud services, or discontinue use of the product if mitigations are unavailable.
vulnerabilities.cisa_vuln_name: Aquasecurity Trivy Embedded Malicious Code Vulnerability

identities.name: Aqua Security
identities.description: Vendor of the Trivy open-source vulnerability scanner, whose release infrastructure and GitHub Actions were the initial point of compromise in the supply chain attack.

identities.name: Checkmarx
identities.description: Vendor of the KICS (Keeping Infrastructure as Code Secure) tool. A GitHub Action for KICS was compromised by TeamPCP as part of the cascading attack.

identities.name: BerriAI
identities.description: The organization behind the LiteLLM project. Malicious versions of LiteLLM were published to PyPI to steal user credentials.

identities.name: Python Package Index (PyPI)
identities.description: The software repository for Python, which was abused by the attackers to distribute malicious versions of the 'litellm' package.

identities.name: GitHub
identities.description: The platform hosting the compromised GitHub Actions for Trivy and Checkmarx KICS. The attackers manipulated Git tags on this platform.

identities.name: OpenAI
identities.description: Provider of LLM services. Its API keys were a primary target for theft from users of the compromised LiteLLM package.

identities.name: Anthropic
identities.description: Provider of LLM services. Its API keys were a primary target for theft from users of the compromised LiteLLM package.

identities.name: Google
identities.description: Provider of the Gemini LLM service. Its API keys were a primary target for theft from users of the compromised LiteLLM package.

infrastructure.name: TeamPCP C2 Server
infrastructure.description: An attacker-controlled server at models.litellm.cloud used to receive exfiltrated AI API keys and other stolen credentials from compromised LiteLLM instances.

indicators.name: TeamPCP LiteLLM C2 Domain
indicators.x_netsecio_sigma_rule:
title: TeamPCP LiteLLM C2 DNS Query
id: 7c5a8d3e-1b9f-4e2a-8c6d-9f8a7b6c5d4e
status: experimental
description: Detects DNS queries to the attacker-controlled C2 domain used in the LiteLLM supply chain attack.
references:
  - https://www.kaspersky.com/blog/team-pcp-supply-chain-attack/51531/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-03-26
tags:
  - attack.t1071.004
  - attack.command_and_control
falsepositives:
  - None expected.
level: high
logsource:
  category: dns_query
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    QueryName|contains:
      - 'models.litellm.cloud'
  condition: selection
indicators.description: Detects network communication to the C2 domain models.litellm.cloud, used for exfiltrating stolen credentials in the TeamPCP supply chain attack.

indicators.name: Installation of Malicious LiteLLM Versions
indicators.pattern:
title: Installation of Malicious LiteLLM PyPI Packages
id: 8d9e0f1a-2b3c-4d5e-8f9a-0b1c2d3e4f5a
status: experimental
description: Detects the installation of specific trojanized versions of the LiteLLM package (1.82.7, 1.82.8) via pip.
references:
  - https://www.kaspersky.com/blog/team-pcp-supply-chain-attack/51531/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-03-26
tags:
  - attack.t1195.002
  - attack.initial_access
falsepositives:
  - Unlikely, as these are specific malicious versions.
level: critical
logsource:
  category: process_creation
  product: windows
detection:
  selection_pip:
    Image|endswith:
      - '\pip.exe'
      - '\pip3.exe'
    CommandLine|contains|all:
      - 'install'
      - 'litellm=='
  selection_version:
    CommandLine|contains:
      - '1.82.7'
      - '1.82.8'
  condition: all of selection_*
indicators.description: Detects command-line activity corresponding to the installation of the malicious LiteLLM versions 1.82.7 and 1.82.8.

indicators.name: Malicious LiteLLM File proxy_server.py
indicators.x_netsecio_sigma_rule:
title: Malicious LiteLLM File Artifact
id: 9e0f1a2b-3c4d-5e8f-9a0b-1c2d3e4f5a6b
status: experimental
description: Detects the presence of proxy_server.py, which contained the malicious code in the compromised LiteLLM package. This rule is best used with path constraints to reduce false positives.
references:
  - https://www.kaspersky.com/blog/team-pcp-supply-chain-attack/51531/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-03-26
tags:
  - attack.t1195.002
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate files with the same name in different applications.
level: medium
logsource:
  category: file_event
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    TargetFilename|endswith: '\proxy_server.py'
  condition: selection
indicators.description: Detects the presence of the file `proxy_server.py`, which was identified as the container for malicious code in the trojanized LiteLLM packages.

relationships.target_name: Compromise Software Dependencies and Development Tools
Command and Scripting Interpreter: Unix Shell
